purpose: Canonical supply-chain attack versions required for publisher-pattern validation (sub-step 4). These versions were unpublished from the npm registry so they cannot be collected by the VPS crawler. Reconstructed from public postmortems. Every data field carries one or more source citations with URL + direct quote. Ground rules from 2026-04-19 review apply: per-field citation, source hierarchy (registry_data > direct_quote > corroboration > inference_weaker), no general knowledge without citation, null for unavailable with UNAVAILABLE source noting why.

source_type_legend.direct_quote: Verbatim quote from a named postmortem page, accessed 2026-04-19
source_type_legend.corroboration: Independent second postmortem source stating the same fact
source_type_legend.inference_weaker: Inferred from a source rather than stated (flag per review rule #2)
source_type_legend.unavailable: Field value is null because no public source states it; reason documented

records.reconstruction_notes: Unpublished from npm registry on 2026-03-31 after ~3h live window. Packument 'time' map retains publish timestamp; versions subtree no longer contains the body. Package.json dependency delta inferred from Datadog + Elastic postmortems plus legitimate adjacent version 1.14.0 retrieved from the registry. axios@1.14.0 baseline data verified live against npm registry on 2026-04-19 (scripts list in citation is an exact 18/18 key match; no install/preinstall/postinstall hooks present).

sources.quote: packument returned for this name
Malicious axios@1.14.1 published from the jasonsaayman account
Malicious axios@1.14.1 published from the jasonsaayman account
axios@1.14.1 published by compromised account

preserved _npmUser excerpt from the unpublished 1.14.1 packument body: {"name": "jasonsaayman", "email": "ifstap@proton.me"} (also reproduced in .versions["1.14.1"].maintainers array alongside unchanged entries)
The maintainer email changed from jasonsaayman@gmail[.]com — present on all prior legitimate releases — to ifstap@proton[.]me on the malicious versions
1.14.1 absent from versions subtree (unpublished); dist.integrity not retrievable
The 1.14.1 source code is identical to 1.14.0. The only meaningful change to package.json was a new dependency: "plain-crypto-js": "^4.2.1"

scripts object on baseline 1.14.0 contains only lifecycle/tooling entries (test, build, lint, prepare, preversion, version, examples, start, fix, test:vitest, test:module:cjs, test:module:esm, test:smoke:cjs:vitest, test:smoke:esm:vitest, test:vitest:unit, test:vitest:watch, test:vitest:browser, test:vitest:browser:headless) — no install, preinstall, or postinstall keys

The analysis centers on the dropper's behavior within the dependency's postinstall hook, not axios's own configuration (attack path is plain-crypto-js@4.2.1's postinstall, not any axios-native script)
For 1.14.1 the trustedPublisher field is absent. This version was published directly from the jasonsaayman account session, not through the GitHub Actions workflow
Direct CLI publish — None (no provenance attestations)
The only meaningful change to package.json was a new dependency: "plain-crypto-js": "^4.2.1"
dependencies: form-data ^4.0.5, proxy-from-env ^2.1.0, follow-redirects ^1.15.11 (baseline legitimate 1.14.0; Datadog states only plain-crypto-js was added)

records.reconstruction_notes: Unpublished after discovery in November 2018. Packument 'time' map retains publish timestamp; versions subtree no longer contains the body. publisher_email on the malicious version is not directly observable (unpublished) — inferred from registry-observed email on adjacent versions plus postmortem statement that right9ctrl (same account) published 3.3.6. Attack mechanism was require-time execution of minified code in flatmap-stream, not npm install hooks.

sources.quote: packument returned for this name
September 9, 2018: created a new minor event-stream release 3.3.6
This package was added as a direct dependency of the event-stream package by a new maintainer on September 9, 2018, in version 3.3.6 (date corroboration only; ms precision from registry)
@right9ctrl ... After he adds it at almost the exact same time the injection is added to flatmap-stream, he bumps the version and publishes
new maintainer ... on September 9, 2018, in version 3.3.6
_npmUser: {name: right9ctrl, email: right9ctrl@outlook.com} on legitimate adjacent version 3.3.5 (4 days before 3.3.6)
_npmUser: {name: right9ctrl, email: right9ctrl@outlook.com} on adjacent version 4.0.0 (post-takeover) — same account, same email
right9ctrl ... took over maintainership (confirms same-account takeover path; exact email on the unpublished 3.3.6 body is not directly observable)
3.3.6 absent from versions subtree (unpublished); dist.integrity not retrievable

flatmap-stream version 0.1.1 included the malicious attack in its minified source code (attack triggered at require-time in the dependency's JS, not via any install/preinstall/postinstall hook on event-stream itself)

scripts object on legitimate adjacent version 3.3.5 (published 4 days before 3.3.6): {prepublish, test, test_tap} — no install, preinstall, or postinstall keys. Snyk confirms the takeover modified only the dependency list, and npm blog confirms flatmap-stream was the only added dep

3.3.6 absent from versions subtree; provenance field not retrievable. npm provenance attestation feature (_attestations / trustedPublisher) was introduced in April 2023 — post-dates the 2018 incident — so the field was not observable on any 2018 publish by design
The malicious package was version 0.1.1 of flatmap-stream. This package was added as a direct dependency of the event-stream package by a new maintainer on September 9, 2018, in version 3.3.6

dependencies: duplexer ^0.1.1, from ^0.1.7, map-stream 0.0.7, pause-stream ^0.0.11, split ^1.0.1, stream-combiner ^0.2.2, through ^2.3.8 (baseline legitimate 3.3.5, published 4 days before 3.3.6; flatmap-stream was the added dep per npm blog)

records.reconstruction_notes: Unpublished on 2021-10-22 after ~4h live window. Attack was same-account takeover (maintainer 'faisalman' npm account hijacked); per the GATE CONTRACT this package is a deliberate NEGATIVE fixture — publisher-pattern signals should NOT fire because publisher identity is unchanged. publisher_email inferred from registry-observed email on adjacent legitimate versions (0.7.28 pre-attack, 0.7.30 post-fix) plus postmortem statement that the maintainer's own account was hijacked.
